PHOL 427: Physiological Contributors to Aviation Mishaps

TransAsia 222: Interactive Case-Analysis Simulator

Master of Science in Aerospace Physiology Program
Department of Physiology and Biophysics, School of Medicine, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio, USA
Spring 2026
Simulation & Analytics by Dr. Joannes Paulus Tolentino Hernandez, Mar 29, 2026 @ 1:58 PM EDT
Contact:

This simulator is designed to visualize how human factors, operational themes, flight-path context, injury distribution, and event sequencing interact during the TransAsia GE222 accident. Its objective is to help users interpret relationships across categories and themes, monitor critical warning signals, follow the accident timeline, and connect the network structure to the underlying report summary and seat-specific outcome distribution. To use the simulator, begin with the influence network on the left to identify dominant category-theme connections, then adjust the theme, category, and minimum-weight controls to refine what is displayed. Next, review the warning monitor and timeline feed on the right to track how risk signals and event progression align with the filtered network. Finally, use the seat-specific outcome image and report summary below to connect the visual analytics to the aviation mishap.

SOURCE: Aviation Safety Council. (2016). Aviation occurrence report: TransAsia Airways Flight GE222 ATR72-212A, B-22810 impacted terrain and collided with a residential area northeast of the threshold of runway 20 at Magong Airport (ASC-AOR-16-01-002). https://www.ttsb.gov.tw/media/3194/asc-aor-16-01-002-en.pdf
Proposed Seat-Specific Outcome Distribution Analysis

The seat-specific outcome distribution should be interpreted as the downstream expression of the accident sequence rather than as a random passenger pattern. This simulation emphasizes that environmental pressure, crew decisions, and procedural breakdown converged to produce the mishap outcome. Timestamp analysis of the video Mayday: Air Disaster (2024, March 22) likewise highlights cognitive, moral, and social judgment themes (Krause, 2003), with the largest thematic clusters centering on crew decisions, environmental pressure, and procedural breakdown. Under this framing, the injury-fatality seat image serves as the final spatial layer of the event chain, showing where survivability remained possible after controlled flight into terrain, structural breakup, and post-impact fire had already redistributed risk unevenly across the cabin.

The report states that the ATR72 passenger cabin had 72 economy seats and that the injury and fatality distribution by seat location was reconstructed from the airline seating plan together with interviews from surviving passengers. It also reports that the aircraft was destroyed by impact forces and a post-impact fire, that only ten passengers survived, and that the fuselage broke apart with some passengers thrown from the cabin or escaping through openings in the structure. Taken together, those details support a seat-specific interpretation centered on localized survivability: seats nearest the most severe impact loads, breakup zones, crush paths, or intense fire exposure would be expected to show disproportionately fatal outcomes, while seats located in pockets of preserved structure or adjacent to usable egress openings would be more likely to appear among the serious-injury survivors.

A proposed reading of the distribution is that variation by seat probably reflects three interacting mechanisms. First, impact energy transfer would not have been uniform across the fuselage, so some seat rows likely absorbed catastrophic deceleration and intrusion while others retained enough living space for survival. Second, post-impact fire and smoke likely converted some otherwise survivable positions into fatal ones, especially in cabin sections where thermal exposure or blocked escape paths escalated rapidly. Third, egress opportunity appears crucial, because the report describes survivors leaving through holes in the fuselage after breakup; this implies that survivorship was partly shaped by whether a passenger's seat location was near a breach, a relatively intact aisle path, or a cabin segment that remained accessible long enough for escape.

In practical analytic terms, the seat image should therefore be read in layers. Rows or clusters with predominantly fatal outcomes may indicate zones of direct terrain impact, secondary collision damage, or severe fire involvement. Mixed clusters of fatalities and serious injuries may mark transitional zones where structural compromise was major but not uniformly unsurvivable. Survivors were primarily concentrated in areas reflecting the intersection of partial structural preservation, shorter evacuation distances, and sufficient time to exit before the post-impact fire became overwhelming. Because nine of the ten surviving passengers were seriously injured, the pattern also suggests that even the more survivable seat locations were still exposed to violent deceleration and cabin disruption, meaning the distribution is better understood as a gradient of extreme risk rather than a simple safe-versus-unsafe split.

Accordingly, the proposed conclusion under the seat image is that seat-specific outcome distribution in GE222 most likely reflects unequal exposure to impact, breakup, fire, and egress access created by the accident sequence. The value of the visualization is not only to show where fatalities occurred, but to connect those locations back to the simulation's causal logic: adverse weather increased workload, crew judgment and procedural compliance degraded, the aircraft descended below minimum safe altitude, and the resulting controlled-flight-into-terrain event produced a cabin environment in which survivability depended heavily on where each occupant was seated relative to structural failure and escape pathways.

Report Summary

On 23 July 2014, TransAsia Airways Flight GE222, an ATR72-212A flying from Kaohsiung to Magong, crashed while carrying out a very high frequency omni-directional radio range (VOR) non-precision approach to Runway 20 in worsening weather. The aircraft struck terrain about 850 meters northeast of the runway threshold and then hit nearby homes. The investigation concluded that the main cause was the crew’s failure to follow the approach procedure. The captain deliberately descended below the minimum descent altitude of 330 feet in instrument meteorological conditions without having the required visual reference to the runway. Both pilots then continued trying to locate the runway visually instead of beginning a missed approach at or before the missed approach point. During this period, the aircraft moved left of the inbound track and its descent rate increased, but the crew did not recognize or correct the unsafe flight path in time, showing a loss of situational awareness. Heavy rain and thunderstorm activity linked to Typhoon Matmo sharply reduced visibility, making it unlikely that the runway environment could be seen. The first officer also failed to challenge the captain’s unsafe decisions and instead assisted the continuation of the descent. Investigators further identified poor crew coordination, weak threat and error management, fatigue, and an airline culture that tolerated procedural non-compliance, which together increased the risk of controlled flight into terrain. (Aviation Safety Council, 2016)
